alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sympatry promoted strong convergence in wing color patterns among swallowtail butterflies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Phenotypic convergence in wing color patterns occurs in sympatric swallowtail butterflies, particularly among distantly related species. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the core finding of sympatric convergence but adds the specificity that it occurs particularly among distantly related species, which narrows/clarifies the scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Divergence in wing color patterns in sympatric species was less frequent and weaker compared to convergence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Divergence in wing color patterns is weaker and restricted to closely related male swallowtail butterflies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the divergence claim by specifying it is restricted to closely related males, narrowing the original (broader) preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The effect of sympatry on convergence was stronger in females than in males, indicating differential selective pressures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The convergence of wing color patterns is stronger among female swallowtail butterflies than among males, indicating differential selective pressures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same directional sex-difference claim but expresses it with higher certainty/strength, so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ecological interactions driven by predation pressures significantly affect trait diversification in Papilionidae. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Ecological interactions driven by predation pressures significantly affect trait diversification in Papilionidae. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same ecological-conclusion statement with no meaningful change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is an interaction between phylogenetic proximity and ecological interactions in sympatry that influences macroevolutionary patterns of phenotypic diversification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is an interaction between phylogenetic proximity and ecological interactions in sympatry that influences macroevolutionary patterns of phenotypic diversification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication carries forward the same conclusion about the interaction between phylogeny and ecology without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The kinetics of TMSD circuits are determined by only a small subpopulation of strongly interacting strands. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The kinetics of TMSD circuits are determined by only a small subpopulation of strongly interacting strands. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this key kinetic result verbatim; no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The equilibration of the circuit with background sequences can lead to up to one order of magnitude difference in reaction speed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The equilibration of the circuit with background sequences can lead to up to 1 order of magnitude difference in reaction speed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same quantitative result; only numeric formatting differs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only the technique of using an additional blocking strand can be employed without sequence constraints to speed up TMSD reactions in random sequence pools. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Only the technique of using an additional blocking strand can be employed without sequence constraints to speed up TMSD reactions in random sequence pools. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same methodological conclusion with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Insights from this study are expected to aid in the construction of TMSD circuits that are robust to molecular noise. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Insights from this study are expected to aid in constructing TMSD circuits that are robust to molecular noise. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this forward-looking contribution with only minor wording changes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PrePCI database comprises over 5 billion predicted interactions between nearly 7 million chemical compounds and 19,797 human proteins. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PrePCI database comprises over 5 billion predicted interactions between 6.8 million chemical compounds and 19,797 human proteins. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same contribution with a slightly more precise compound count (6.8 million vs "nearly 7 million"), so the claim is clarified but substantively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PrePCI algorithm performs well under different validations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not present an explicit, matching claim about overall algorithm performance under different validations, so the preprint performance claim is not represented.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PrePCI can be queried by entering a UniProt ID for a protein to obtain a list of compounds predicted to bind to it. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PrePCI database can be queried to obtain a list of compounds predicted to bind to a protein or a list of proteins predicted to bind to a compound. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the query capability and explicitly adds the reverse query (compound→proteins), broadening and clarifying the original query claim while increasing certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A likelihood ratio for the binding of compounds to proteins is obtained from naïve Bayesian statistics. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The likelihood ratio for binding is derived from machine learning when metrics exceed threshold values. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication changes the methodological basis from naïve Bayesian statistics to a machine-learning-derived approach with thresholds, so the claim is revised methodologically and expressed with higher confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The shape based coarse graining (SBCG) method employs unsupervised machine learning to construct coarse molecules that perfectly represent atomistic topologies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SBCG2 preserves atomistic details that maintain assembly characteristics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication tones down the absolute "perfect" representation claim to stating preservation of atomistic details relevant to assemblies, representing a revision and narrowing of the original assertion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SBCG provides ample control over model granularity and includes a quantitative metric for selection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We present a method of granularity selection based on charge density Fourier Shell Correlation. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication describes a specific quantitative granularity-selection metric consistent with the preprint's claim, clarifying the method used.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SBCG is compatible with the CHARMM Hamiltonian and enables molecular sampling of over a microsecond per day using GPU-accelerated NAMD3. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report compatibility with CHARMM or the specific GPU-enabled sampling performance, so this implementation/performance claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SBCG method requires minimal user input and lacks any ad hoc interactions between protein domains. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make the minimal-user-input or absence-of-ad-hoc-interactions claim, so this preprint conclusion is not represented.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Targeted Memory Reactivation (TMR) can improve various types of memory. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Targeted memory reactivation (TMR) can improve memory and may be useful for cognitive enhancement and clinical therapy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication echoes the core claim that TMR can improve memory and extends potential applications, thereby clarifying and broadening the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SleepStim system can effectively accomplish automated TMR in a home environment. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The SleepStim system can effectively accomplish automated TMR in the home environment. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about SleepStim's capacity for home automated TMR with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Low-intensity stimuli during TMR produced memory benefits, while high-intensity stimuli did not. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The TMR effect was not significant in an experiment with 61 participants but varied systematically with stimulus intensity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a more nuanced, sample-size-dependent result indicating systematic dependence on intensity rather than the categorical low-benefit/high-no-benefit statement, so the original claim is revised and somewhat tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TMR reliably improved spatial memory when stimulus intensity was limited. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In an experiment with 24 participants, TMR reliably improved spatial memory when stimulus intensity was limited. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same reliable spatial-memory benefit but adds the specific experiment/sample context, clarifying the original result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Avoiding sleep disruption is critical for TMR benefits. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Avoiding sleep disruption is critical for achieving TMR benefits. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same conclusion emphasizing the importance of avoiding sleep disruption for TMR efficacy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning model accurately predicts the extent of promoter proximal Pol II pausing from large scale genome and transcriptome binding maps. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning model accurately predicts the extent of promoter-proximal Pol II pausing from large-scale genome and transcriptome binding maps. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the model prediction claim with essentially identical wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model demonstrates high accuracy and generalizability, validated on an independent cell line, indicating its cell line agnostic character. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrates high accuracy and generalizability, revealing its cell line agnostic character. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same claim about accuracy, generalizability, and cell-line-agnostic behavior with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identifies 16 previously unknown 7SK ncRNA interacting RNA-binding proteins that are predictive of pausing. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identifies 16 previously unknown 7SK ncRNA interacting RNA-binding proteins that are predictive of pausing. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces this specific discovery claim verbatim and with the same confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The research quantifies the interconnection of Pol II pausing with other RNA processing steps. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The assessment of feature contributions allows for the quantification of the predictive effects of various factors on Pol II pausing. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that feature-contribution assessment enables quantification of various factors' predictive effects, clarifying the preprint's more general statement about interconnections.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The work provides a framework to further understand the regulation of the critical early steps in transcriptional elongation. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The work provides a framework to enhance understanding of the regulation of the critical early steps in transcriptional elongation. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same contribution framing (a framework to improve understanding) with equivalent wording and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DART provides a complete end-to-end sequence of tools that form a design-build-test-learn loop for synthetic genetic networks. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DART provides rational selection and refinement of genetic parts to construct and test a circuit. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames DART more narrowly as selection/refinement of parts for construct-and-test, removing the explicit claim of a full end-to-end design-build-test-learn loop, thus revising and narrowing the original scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Design Assemble part of DART improves on previous techniques by screening up to thousands of network topologies for robust performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Design Assemble part of DART improves on previous techniques by screening up to thousands of network topologies for robust performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this result verbatim, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The execution of DART tested the predictions of the design tools regarding robust and reproducible performance under different experimental conditions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The execution of DART tested the predictions of the design tools regarding robust and reproducible performance under different experimental conditions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same execution/testing claim unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Evidence is presented that a more complex build may impart more robustness and reproducibility across experimental conditions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Evidence is presented that, in some cases, a more complex build may impart more robustness and reproducibility across experimental conditions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The full model explained approximately 40% of the variance in migration rate over the past 56 generations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed machine learning method explained approximately 40% of the variance in migration rate over the past 56 generations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical result and timescale are the same; publication adds the method descriptor but does not change the claim content.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Precipitation, minimum temperature of the coldest month, and altitude were identified as the most important variables affecting migration rates. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Precipitation, minimum temperature of the coldest month, and elevation were identified as the variables with the highest impact on migration rates. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Signatures of positive selection related to metabolism and disease were found in a subgroup of populations living at relatively high altitudes in Ethiopia. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study found signatures of positive selection related to metabolism and disease among Ethiopian populations, despite not identifying well-known genes related to high elevation. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The environment has notably shaped the migration and adaptation of human populations in eastern Africa. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The environment has influenced the migration and adaptation of human populations in eastern Africa. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The remaining variance in genetic structure is likely due to cultural factors not captured in the model. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The remaining variance in genetic structure is likely due in part to cultural or other factors not captured in the model. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication keeps the attribution to cultural factors but explicitly broadens to include 'other factors', slightly expanding the scope without changing expressed confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LoGoFunc is an ensemble machine learning method developed for predicting pathogenic gain-of-function, pathogenic loss-of-function, and neutral genetic variants. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LoGoFunc is a machine learning method developed for predicting pathogenic gain-of-function, pathogenic loss-of-function, and neutral genetic variants. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both statements describe the same method and prediction targets; wording difference (ensemble) is minor and does not change the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GOF and LOF variants exhibit contrasting enrichments in protein structural and functional regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not present an explicit claim about contrasting enrichments in structural/functional regions, so this specific result appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LOF variants are more likely to disrupt canonical splicing as indicated by splicing-related features employed by the model. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state the splicing-disruption enrichment result; any related mention of features is more general and does not directly support this specific claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Phenome-wide association studies (PheWAS) identified strong associations between relevant phenotypes and high-confidence predicted GOF and LOF variants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not include the PheWAS association result, so this preprint claim appears absent from the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LoGoFunc outperforms other tools trained solely to predict pathogenicity or general variant impact for the identification of pathogenic GOF and LOF variants. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LoGoFunc outperforms other tools that are trained solely to predict pathogenicity in identifying pathogenic gain-of-function and loss-of-function variants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the performance advantage claim directly and with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The method LoGoFunc is trained on a broad range of gene-, protein-, and variant-level features that describe diverse biological characteristics. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an explicit claim about the feature set used in training that was not stated as a separate claim in the preprint, so this is an added publication claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Existing DFL predictors based on machine learning techniques tend to predict ordered residues as DFLs, leading to a high false positive rate and low prediction accuracy. (certainty=high, type=limitation)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TransDFL consistently outperforms other existing DFL predictors with higher accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TransDFL consistently outperformed other existing DFL predictors with higher accuracy in predicting DFLs. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The RFPR-IDP predictor was pre-trained with IDR sequences to learn the general features between IDRs and DFLs, which helps reduce false positives in the ordered regions. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The RFPR-IDP predictor was pre-trained with IDR sequences to learn the general features between IDRs and DFLs, which helps reduce false positives in the ordered regions. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TransDFL was fine-tuned with DFL sequences to capture the specific features of DFLs. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TransDFL was fine-tuned with DFL sequences to capture the specific features of DFLs. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The fine-tuning claim is restated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients with or without lymph node metastasis (LNM) have different molecular signatures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An effective 55-proteins prediction model for lymph node metastasis in T1 colorectal cancer achieved an AUC of 1.00 in the training cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes a concrete predictive model and performance, which implies different molecular signatures between patient groups; the mapping is inferential rather than verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The 55-proteins prediction model achieved an impressive AUC of 1.00 in the training cohort, 0.96 in VC1 and 0.93 in VC2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An effective 55-proteins prediction model for lymph node metastasis in T1 colorectal cancer achieved an AUC of 1.00 in the training cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the training AUC but omits the validation AUCs reported in the preprint, narrowing the presented scope of performance metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The 9-protein classifier achieved an AUC of 0.824, and the calibration plot was excellent. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A simplified classifier with nine proteins achieved an AUC of 0.824 and performed excellently in two external validation cohorts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The nine-protein classifier's AUC is identical and the publication emphasizes successful external validation consistent with the preprint's positive calibration statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Five biomarkers could predict LNM by the IHC score, with an AUC of 0.825. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The IHC score of five proteins was used to build a predict model with an AUC of 0.825. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The IHC five-protein predictive model and AUC are reported the same way in publication and preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RHOT2 silence significantly enhanced migration and invasion of colon cancer cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: RHOT2 silence significantly enhanced migration and invasion of colon cancer cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The cellular functional finding for RHOT2 is stated identically in both versions.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The study can facilitate the individualized prediction of lymph node metastasis in patients with T1 colorectal cancer. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an explicit translational/conclusion statement on facilitating individualized prediction that is not present as a distinct claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A model established on dmPFC/dACC metabolites explains decisions to exert high mental effort and sensitivity to mental effort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A model established on dmPFC/dACC metabolites explains decisions to exert high mental effort and sensitivity to mental effort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core modelling result is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Glutamate, aspartate, and lactate in dmPFC/dACC were identified as key discriminative metabolites predictive of mental effort choices. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Glutamate, aspartate, and lactate in dmPFC/dACC were identified as key discriminative metabolites predictive of mental effort choices. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The metabolite-specific predictive finding is restated verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Anterior insula metabolites did not significantly relate to effort-related decisions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Anterior insula metabolites did not significantly relate to effort-related decisions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The negative result for anterior insula metabolites is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Glutamine and lactate levels between the periphery and the dmPFC/dACC were correlated, suggesting a metabolic link between peripheral and central biomarkers of effort. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Glutamine and lactate levels between the periphery (plasma) and the dmPFC/dACC were correlated, suggesting a metabolic link between peripheral and central biomarkers of effort. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The reported correlation and suggested peripheral-central link are preserved with the same interpretation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our findings provide novel insights into the neurometabolic underpinnings of motivated behavior and propose novel biomarkers for mental effort-based decision-making. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings provide novel insights into the neurometabolic underpinnings of motivated behavior and propose novel biomarkers for mental effort-based decision-making. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Proximity to capillaries has a greater impact on mitochondrial energy conversion and distribution in skeletal muscle than the sarcolemma alone. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Proximity to capillaries has a greater impact on resting mitochondrial energy conversion and distribution in skeletal muscle than the sarcolemma alone. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the word 'resting', which clarifies the context but preserves the original conclusion and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PVM were more compact and had greater mitochondrial connectivity compared to both IFM and SSM. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Paravascular mitochondria are larger, rounder, and have more physical connections to neighboring mitochondria compared to interfibrillar and subsarcolemmal mitochondria. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication modifies morphological description (from 'compact' to 'larger, rounder') while retaining the connectivity comparison, indicating a revision in the morphological claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Functionally, PVM had similar or greater basal NADH flux compared to SSM and IFM, despite a more oxidized NADH pool and a greater membrane potential. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Paravascular mitochondria have greater activation of oxidative phosphorylation than interfibrillar mitochondria at rest, although this is not regulated by calcium. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the functional result as greater oxidative phosphorylation activation specifically vs interfibrillar mitochondria at rest and omits the comparison to SSM and the NADH wording, narrowing and revising the original functional claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Embeddings can capture detailed information about disease states and have been qualitatively shown to be useful in genetic discovery. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Embeddings can capture detailed information about disease states and have been qualitatively shown to be useful in genetic discovery. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this claim verbatim, preserving meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EmbedGEM is a framework designed to systematically evaluate the utility of embeddings in genetic discovery by comparing them along the axes of heritability and disease relevance. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EmbedGEM is a framework that systematically evaluates the utility of embeddings in genetic discovery by comparing them along the axes of heritability and disease relevance. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the framework description without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The development of EmbedGEM is motivated by embeddings but is generally applicable to multivariate traits and can be extended to accommodate additional metrics. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EmbedGEM can be extended to accommodate additional metrics along the evaluation axes beyond embeddings. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the extendability explicit; this clarifies rather than contradicting the preprint's general applicability claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Greater disease relevance does not automatically follow from greater heritability. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Greater disease relevance does not automatically follow from greater heritability. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this conclusion verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AAV2 has emerged as a prominent candidate in gene therapy due to its non-pathogenic nature in humans. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AAV2 are effective candidates for gene therapy due to their non-pathogenic nature and safety in manipulation. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds emphasis on safety in manipulation, clarifying but not changing the core conclusion about AAV2 as candidates.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The efficacy of AAV2 as gene therapy vectors is reliant on their ability to construct a capsid that can breach the nucleus of the target cell. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The efficacy of AAV2 as gene therapy vectors is dependent on their ability to construct a capsid that can breach the nucleus of target cells. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this mechanistic conclusion with equivalent wording and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A biophysically-inspired model has been introduced to predict the viability of genetic variants in Deep Mutational Scanning experiments. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A biophysically-inspired model has been introduced to predict the viability of genetic variants in deep mutational scanning experiments. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the model contribution in essentially the same terms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model is designed to provide quantitative forecasts for the log-selectivity of variants and to classify sequences into viable and non-viable classes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model is designed to provide quantitative forecasts for the log-selectivity of variants and to classify sequences into viable and non-viable classes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the stated predictive and classification capabilities of the model.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The StELa double clustering method effectively clusters protein structures based on the Ramachandran plot. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The published paper does not assert clustering based on the Ramachandran plot; that specific claim is not present in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: StELa identifies nearly all the minima from the corresponding free energy landscapes (FELs) in molecular dynamics simulations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The StELa double-clustering method effectively identifies many of the minima and relevant energy states around the free energy landscapes from molecular dynamics simulations of intrinsically disordered proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the identification of minima but softens 'nearly all' to 'many' and emphasizes relevant energy states, tempering the original strength of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GROMOS produces a large number of clusters that cover the entire FEL, but does not identify all minima. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The RMSD-based clustering algorithm produces a large number of clusters that fail to distinguish between states in the free energy landscape. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the same observation more directly (many clusters that fail to distinguish states), clarifying the practical shortcoming of the RMSD-based/GROMOS approach.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CATS fails to sample well the FEL for long intrinsically disordered proteins and globular protein fragments, missing important minima. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CATS does not adequately sample the free energy landscapes for long intrinsically disordered proteins and fragments from globular proteins. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the claim about inadequate sampling and states it with higher confidence, effectively amplifying the original result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both mouse models showed increasingly stronger gaiting deficits. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gradual adult-onset Purkinje cell degeneration leads to progressive gaiting deficits in mice. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same core result (progressive gait deficits) observed in the mouse models, framed in terms of Purkinje cell degeneration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sequence with which gaiting parameters deteriorated depended on the specific mutation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The sequence of deterioration in gaiting parameters differs between mouse models with different mechanisms of Purkinje cell degeneration. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates that the order of parameter deterioration varies between models with different mechanisms, matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Gradual loss of Purkinje cell function can lead to a complex pattern of loss of function over time. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The pattern of loss of function due to gradual loss of Purkinje cell function is complex and depends on the specifics of the pathological mechanisms involved. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the conclusion but adds dependency on specific pathological mechanisms and reports lower certainty, clarifying and tempering the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The variability in gaiting deficits will also be present in disease progression in patients. (certainty=medium, type=hypothesis)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The variability in gait ataxia progression observed in mouse models may also be present in disease progression in patients. (certainty=low, type=hypothesis)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same hypothesis but more cautiously ('may also be present') and with lower stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our findings will facilitate the study of therapeutic interventions in mice. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The methods developed in this study allow for the quantification of subtle changes in locomotor abilities in mice. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the contribution more concretely (method for quantification of subtle locomotor changes) rather than the broader claim about facilitating therapeutic studies, so the contribution has been revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our proposed approach leverages the Delta method to derive the variance and covariance of AUC values, enabling a comprehensive and efficient comparative significance test. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed approach using the Delta method enables a comprehensive and efficient comparative significance test for AUC values. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution and methodology (Delta method for AUC comparative tests) with essentially identical scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method offers notable advantages over DeLong’s method, including reduced computation time (up to 150-fold), making it suitable for large-scale analyses. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our method reduces computation time for significance tests by up to 150-fold compared to DeLong’s method. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same quantitative speed-up claim versus DeLong’s method with unchanged scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our approach allows for a direct one-to-one mapping between AUC and R 2 values for comparative significance tests, providing enhanced insights into the relationship between these measures. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method allows for a direct one-to-one mapping between AUC and R2 values, enhancing the interpretation of these measures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both statements claim a direct one-to-one mapping between AUC and R2; the publication frames it as improving interpretation but preserves the core claim and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We validated our proposed approach through simulations and applied it to real data comparing PRSs for diabetes and coronary artery disease (CAD) prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach was validated through simulations and applied to real data comparing PRSs for diabetes and coronary artery disease in a cohort of 28,880 European individuals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains validation and real-data application but adds cohort-size and population detail, which clarifies rather than contradicts the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This advancement contributes to the assessment of genetic risk factors and personalized disease prediction, supporting better healthcare decision-making. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state the broader health-decision or personalized prediction impact expressed in the preprint, so this higher-level conclusion appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We have discovered the existence of cancer type-specific evolutionary signatures associated with different disease outcomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Recurring sequences of genomic alterations can highlight repeated evolutionary processes with significant implications for predicting cancer progression. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both claims assert that recurring, cancer-type-specific evolutionary patterns have implications for disease outcomes/progression; the publication frames it slightly more generally but preserves the core result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ASCETIC framework extracts cancer (sub)type-specific single-nucleotide variants evolutionary signatures from various sequencing data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework ASCETIC successfully extracts cancer’s evolutionary signatures tied to distinct disease outcomes from various sequencing technologies. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same contribution (ASCETIC extracts evolutionary signatures across sequencing technologies) with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The extracted evolutionary signatures are associated with clusters of patients that have statistically significant different prognoses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ASCETIC reveals subtype-specific single-nucleotide variant signatures associated with distinct prognostic clusters in multiple cancer datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication directly restates that extracted signatures associate with distinct prognostic patient clusters across datasets; content, scope and certainty are preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our analyses provided evidence of the robustness and generalizability of the identified evolutionary patterns. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Validations on several datasets demonstrate the robustness and generalizability of the extracted signatures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens the claim by reporting validations across several datasets and elevates the certainty from medium to high, clarifying and amplifying generalizability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: λ-antibodies are on average at a higher risk of poor developability compared to κ-antibodies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Human λ-antibodies on average have a higher risk of developability issues than κ-antibodies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same average-difference result between λ and κ antibodies with unchanged wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Over 40% of single-cell sequenced human λ-antibodies are flagged by TAP for risk-prone patches of surface hydrophobicity, compared to around 11% of human κ-antibodies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A sizeable proportion of λ-antibodies are assigned lower-risk profiles by TAP and should represent more tractable candidates for therapeutic development. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific negative numeric claim about high proportions flagged in the preprint is not reproduced; the publication emphasizes that a sizeable proportion are assigned lower-risk profiles—a substantive change in the claim's content.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A substantial proportion of natural λ-antibodies are assigned more moderate risk profiles by TAP, indicating they could be more tractable candidates for therapeutic development. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A sizeable proportion of λ-antibodies are assigned lower-risk profiles by TAP and should represent more tractable candidates for therapeutic development. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core conclusion that a notable fraction of λ-antibodies have lower-risk profiles but reports it with higher certainty and as a stronger result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The updated TAP tool provides a rational approach to incorporate more diversity into the initial pool of immunotherapeutic candidates. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The updated Therapeutic Antibody Profiler (TAP) tool enables a rational approach to incorporate more diversity into the initial pool of immunotherapeutic candidates. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the contribution of the updated TAP tool enabling more diverse candidate selection with similar scope and certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a functional cost to neglecting λ-antibodies as potential lead candidates in drug discovery pipelines. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This conclusion about the functional cost of neglecting λ-antibodies is presented in the publication but has no counterpart in the preprint, so it is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Coordinate descent and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) tend to perform comparably in predicting driver mutation presence or absence from gene expression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: liblinear and SGD tended to perform comparably after model selection and tuning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports comparable performance between the two optimizer approaches after model selection, matching the preprint's result (terminology differs slightly but refers to the same methods).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Liblinear models required more extensive tuning of regularization strength and performed best for high model sparsities, but did not require selection of a learning rate parameter. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: liblinear models required more extensive tuning of regularization strength and performed best for high model sparsities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the core point about liblinear tuning and sparsity performance; the publication omits the detail about learning-rate requirement (a minor clarification of wording).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SGD models required tuning of the learning rate to perform well, but generally performed more robustly across different model sparsities as regularization strength decreased. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SGD models required tuning of the learning rate to perform well but generally performed more robustly across different model sparsities as regularization strength decreased. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same behavior and requirements for SGD with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The choice of optimizers should be clearly reported as part of the model selection and validation process. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The choice of optimizers should be clearly reported as a part of the model selection and validation process. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication directly reproduces the recommendation about reporting optimizer choice with unchanged wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The normalisation tools can correct for time-dependent drift in PTR-MS data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Normalisation tools can correct for time-dependent drift in PTR-MS data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same experimental result about normalisation correcting time-dependent PTR-MS drift with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Probabilistic Quotient Normalisation and Normalisation using Optimal Selection of Multiple Internal Standards provided the best corrections for both cohorts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Probabilistic quotient normalisation and normalisation using optimal selection of multiple internal standards provided the best corrections for both cohorts in the study. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same comparative result identifying the two best normalization methods for the cohorts.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Normalisation improved the diagnostic performance of machine learning models for COVID-19 diagnosis, significantly increasing sensitivity, specificity, and area under the ROC curve. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Normalisation improved the diagnostic performance of machine learning models, significantly increasing sensitivity, specificity, and area under the ROC curve for the diagnosis of COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication claim the same significant improvement in diagnostic metrics for COVID-19 after normalisation with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Adding an appropriate normalisation step during the processing of PTR-MS data allows significant improvements in the predictive performance of statistical models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study highlights the importance of adding an appropriate normalisation step during the processing of PTR-MS data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the recommendation but phrases it more as a highlighted importance and with slightly lower certainty (medium vs preprint's high), thus clarifying and tempering the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Unsupervised clustering approaches reveal emergent state-like structure in spontaneous electrophysiological data that recapitulate arousal-related cortical states as indexed by behavioral indicators.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Unsupervised clustering approaches reveal emergent state-like structure in spontaneous electrophysiological data that recapitulate arousal-related cortical states as indexed by behavioral indicators.
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication texts are effectively identical in content and scope, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HSMMs enable cortical state inferences in a real-time context by modeling the temporal dynamics of cortical state switching.
alignments.pub_claim_text: HSMMs enable cortical state inferences in a real-time context by modeling the temporal dynamics of cortical state switching.
alignments.rationale: The claim is carried forward verbatim with the same emphasis on temporal modeling for real-time inference.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using HSMMs provides robustness to state estimates arising from noisy, sequential electrophysiological data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using HSMMs provides robustness to state estimates arising from noisy, sequential electrophysiological data.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result claim about robustness with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work represents the first implementation of a real-time software tool for continuously decoding cortical states with high temporal resolution (40 ms).
alignments.pub_claim_text: This work represents the first implementation of a real-time software tool for continuously decoding cortical states with high temporal resolution (40 ms).
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The software tools provided can facilitate our understanding of how cortical states dynamically modulate cortical function on a moment-by-moment basis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The software tools provided can facilitate our understanding of how cortical states dynamically modulate cortical function on a moment-by-moment basis.
alignments.rationale: The concluding statement about the utility of the software tools is preserved verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We detected genetic signatures associated with sensitivity or resistance to specific agents in AML.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We detected genetic signatures associated with sensitivity or resistance to specific agents.
alignments.rationale: The publication carries the same detection claim; the omission of the explicit 'in AML' is likely stylistic given the paper context, so meaning is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models demonstrated high accuracy in predicting sensitivity to some drugs, such as MEK inhibitors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using the mutation pattern for drug response prediction demonstrates high accuracy in predicting sensitivity to some drug classes, such as MEK inhibitors for RAS-mutated leukemia.
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the claim by specifying the predictive feature (mutation pattern) and a narrower clinical context (RAS-mutated leukemia), making the claim more specific.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of single-cell genomic sequencing unveiled the co-occurrence of variants at the individual cell level, highlighting the presence of distinct sub-clones within AML patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of single-cell genomic sequencing unveiled the co-occurrence of variants at the individual cell level, highlighting the presence of distinct subclones within patients with AML.
alignments.rationale: The content is the same but the publication presents the claim with higher certainty; thus semantic and scope are unchanged but confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our study provides valuable resources for characterizing AML patients and predicting drug sensitivity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: It provides a framework for categorizing patients with AML by mutations that enable drug sensitivity prediction.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the resource claim as a specific framework for mutation-based categorization, clarifying the form of the resource without changing the overall intent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: By tailoring treatment based on individual genetic profiles and functional testing, we envision a future of improved therapeutic strategies for AML patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our study highlights the importance of considering the gene mutation patterns for the prediction of drug response in AML.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's forward-looking clinical vision is softened in the publication to a more modest statement emphasizing importance of mutation patterns for prediction, reducing scope and tempering the confidence in prospective clinical impact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An intelligent scanning methodology based on compressive sensing has been fully implemented on a soft X-ray microscopy beamline.
alignments.pub_claim_text: An intelligent scanning methodology based on compressive sensing has been fully implemented on a soft X-ray microscopy beamline.
alignments.rationale: The implementation claim is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This methodology demonstrated sparse low energy XRF scanning of dynamically chosen regions of interest in combination with STXM.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This methodology demonstrated sparse low energy XRF scanning of dynamically chosen regions of interest in combination with STXM, yielding spectroimaging data in the megapixel-range and in shorter timeframes than previously feasible.
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the result by quantifying data scale and time improvements, clarifying and broadening the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method resulted in sparse megapixel-range low energy XRF spectroimaging through dynamic scanning that was previously not feasible.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This methodology demonstrated sparse low energy XRF scanning of dynamically chosen regions of interest in combination with STXM, yielding spectroimaging data in the megapixel-range and in shorter timeframes than previously feasible.
alignments.rationale: This preprint claim is restated in the publication with the same emphasis on achieving megapixel-range spectroimaging and improved timeframes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method has been successfully applied to various samples, including lung and ovarian human tissues and plant root sections.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method has been successfully applied to various scientific applications in biology, including lung and ovarian human tissues and plant root sections.
alignments.rationale: Application to the same sample types is preserved verbatim; only minor wording broadening to 'scientific applications in biology' is present but not a substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An auxiliary development of a mobile application assists in the selection of specific regions of interest.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A mobile application has been developed to assist users in selecting specific regions of interest easily.
alignments.rationale: The mobile-app development claim is carried forward with equivalent meaning and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tested multivariate approaches produce largely reliable lesion inferences without requiring lesion size consideration.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tested multivariate approaches produce largely reliable lesion inferences without requiring lesion size consideration.
alignments.rationale: The result is reproduced exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analyses showed a considerably better performance of the tested multivariate methods in terms of accuracy and mis-inference error compared to univariate methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analyses showed a considerably better performance of the tested multivariate methods in terms of accuracy and mis-inference error compared to univariate methods.
alignments.rationale: Performance comparisons are unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The univariate approaches of Lesion Symptom Mapping (LSM) and Lesion Symptom Correlation (LSC) mis-inferred substantial contributions from several areas even in the single-region models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The univariate approaches of Lesion Symptom Mapping and Lesion Symptom Correlation mis-inferred substantial contributions from several areas even in the single-region models.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the claim about mis-inference by univariate approaches with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The multivariate approaches of Multi-Area Pattern Prediction (MAPP) and Multi-perturbation Shapley value Analysis (MSA) delivered consistently higher accuracy and specificity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multivariate approaches of Multi-Area Pattern Prediction and Multi-perturbation Shapley value Analysis delivered consistently higher accuracy and specificity.
alignments.rationale: This result about multivariate methods is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We established a novel gene expression similarity-based workflow for the discovery of immune cell type signatures that leverages multiple datasets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We established a novel workflow for the discovery of immune cell type signatures based primarily on gene-versus-gene expression similarity.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the workflow claim with virtually identical scope and emphasis on gene-vs-gene similarity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We obtained similar or better prediction results compared to commonly used methods for cell type assignment in two independent benchmarking datasets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We obtained similar or better prediction results compared to commonly used methods for cell type assignment in independent benchmarking datasets.
alignments.rationale: Benchmarking performance claims are preserved in the publication with the same comparative outcome.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our gene signature set yields higher prediction scores than other published immune cell type gene sets in our random forest approach.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our gene signature set yields higher prediction scores than other published immune cell type gene sets in random forest-based cell type classification.
alignments.rationale: The publication carries the same result about superior prediction scores using the random forest classifier.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrated the quality of our immune cell signatures and their strong performance in a random forest-based cell typing approach.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our gene signature set yields higher prediction scores than other published immune cell type gene sets in random forest-based cell type classification.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the demonstration as quantifiable superior prediction scores; this clarifies the original general statement about quality and strong performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Classifying cells based on our comparably slim sets of genes accompanied by a random forest-based approach facilitates unbiased downstream statistical analyses of differential gene expression for 90% of all genes whose expression profiles have not been used for cell type classification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Classifying cells based on our comparably slim sets of genes accompanied by a random forest-based approach facilitates unbiased downstream statistical analyses of differential gene expression between cell types for significantly more genes compared to previous cell classification algorithms.
alignments.rationale: The publication softens the quantitative claim (from a specific '90%' to a more qualitative 'significantly more genes'), narrowing and tempering the original assertion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Visually similar videos were encoded in overlapping patterns of neural activity in temporal, parietal, and occipital regions, suggesting integration. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Visually similar videos were encoded in overlapping patterns of neural activity in temporal, parietal, and occipital regions, suggesting integration. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication texts are effectively identical in content and certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Encoding processes differentially predicted later reinstatement across the cortex. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Encoding processes differentially predicted later reinstatement across the cortex. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is the same in wording and reported certainty between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Greater differentiation at encoding predicted later reinstatement in visual processing regions in occipital cortex. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Greater differentiation at encoding predicted later reinstatement in visual processing regions in occipital cortex. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No substantive changes in content or confidence; the claim is preserved exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Highly integrated stimuli showed greater reinstatement in higher-level sensory processing regions in temporal and parietal lobes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Highly integrated stimuli showed greater reinstatement in higher-level sensory processing regions in temporal and parietal lobes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same assertion and level of certainty as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Integration in high-level sensory processing regions during encoding predicted greater accuracy and vividness at recall. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Integration in high-level sensory processing regions during encoding predicted greater accuracy and vividness at recall. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This result is repeated verbatim with unchanged certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NEURON is widely used in the neuroscience community to address a broad range of research questions related to neuronal integration and information propagation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Compartmental modeling programs like NEURON are widely used to address research questions related to how neurons integrate synaptic inputs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the broad preprint statement to focus specifically on integration of synaptic inputs, narrowing the original scope while preserving the core point.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NRN-EZ is an interactive application that allows users to specify complex patterns of synaptic input activity for NEURON simulations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NRN-EZ is an interactive application that allows users to specify complex patterns of synaptic input activity for NEURON simulations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution is stated identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The graphical user interface of NRN-EZ helps reduce the learning curve for users without a computer science background. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The graphical user interface of NRN-EZ aims to ease the learning curve for users without a computer science background. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published wording shifts from an asserted effect ("helps reduce") to a stated aim ("aims to ease"), which softens the claim's confidence though the scope remains the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The present study demonstrated that task-based functional connectivity data can be used to generate theoretically valid measures of cognitive reserve. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Task-based functional connectivity data can be used to generate theoretically valid measures of cognitive reserve. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the core result and same certainty; wording differences are minimal.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Three network-strength predicted cognitive reserve measures were generated that accurately predicted the residual measures of unseen participants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model generated summary measures of connectivity strength that accurately predicted a residual measure of cognitive reserve in unseen participants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the description ("summary measures"/singular residual measure) but conveys the same predictive outcome; wording is slightly less specific about number of measures.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The theoretical validity of these measures was established via a positive correlation with a socio-behavioural proxy of cognitive reserve (verbal intelligence) and a positive correlation with global cognition, independent of brain structure. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The theoretical validity of these measures was established via a positive correlation with a socio-behavioural proxy of cognitive reserve (verbal intelligence) and a positive correlation with global cognition, independent of brain structure. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same but the publication reports a lowered level of certainty/interpretation (high -> medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The network-strength predicted measures were not positively associated with a residual measure of cognitive reserve nor with measures of verbal intelligence and global cognition in external test data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The network-strength predicted measures were not positively associated with a residual measure of cognitive reserve nor with measures of verbal intelligence and global cognition. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication omits the qualifier "in external test data," which broadens the context of the negative association statement, and reduces certainty from high to medium.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further work is needed to establish if, and how, measures of cognitive reserve derived from task-based functional connectivity can be applied to independent resting-state data. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Further work is needed to establish if, and how, measures of cognitive reserve derived from task-based functional connectivity can be applied to independent resting-state data. (certainty=low, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation statement is preserved but reported certainty is reduced (medium -> low), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identified 11 quantitative trait loci (QTLs) linked to 7 seed traits in lettuce. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identified 11 QTLs linked to 7 seed traits in lettuce. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is stated identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The three QTLs with the highest LOD scores mapped to linkage group 3, a region associated with domestication traits from wild species. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The three QTLs with the highest LOD scores mapped to a genomic region associated with domestication traits. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication removes the linkage group number and explicit "from wild species" phrasing, making the statement less specific (broader) while preserving the overall result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The oilseed cultivar harbors genes acquired during domestication that control seed shape in the identified genomic region. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The oilseed cultivar harbors genes acquired during domestication that control seed shape. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication drops the explicit reference to the "identified genomic region," making the conclusion less region-specific and therefore broader in scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides genetic evidence that domestication arose, at least in part, by selection for the oilseed type from wild species. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This interpretation about domestication selection is not present in the publication's claims and appears to have been omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The effectiveness of image-based phenotyping was demonstrated to accelerate discoveries of the genetic basis for small morphological features such as seed size and shape. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
